reasoning: The current 30-year fixed mortgage rate (as of April 9, 2026) is 6.37%, down from a local peak of 6.46% the week prior. The FRED statistical model for a 1-week horizon predicts a continuing downward trend (predicted value 6.15%, P(>6.37) = 0.26). Expert consensus supports a drop, with 56% of Bankrate experts expecting a short-term decrease. For the medium term (late 2026 to 2027), institutional forecasts (Fannie Mae, MBA, Wells Fargo) unanimously project a gradual decline to the 5.6%–6.1% range due to anticipated Federal Reserve rate cuts. However, macroeconomic volatility (inflation risks, deficits) means rates could surprise to the upside, so my probabilities do not drop below 0.28. For the longer term (3–10 years), uncertainty significantly widens. Over these horizons, historical mean reversion takes over. Given that 6.37% is relatively elevated compared to the post-2008 average but in line with late 90s/early 00s averages, long-term probabilities slope gracefully upward toward 0.50, but remain slightly below 0.50 because a return to the long-term historical structural norms (which include the ultra-low 2010s) slightly favors a sub-6.37% long-term equilibrium.
